sentences.speaker: RICHARD
sentences.text: Have done thy charm, thou hateful, withered hag.

sentences.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
sentences.text:
And leave out thee? Stay, dog, for thou shalt hear me.
If heaven have any grievous plague in store
Exceeding those that I can wish upon thee,
O, let them keep it till thy sins be ripe
And then hurl down their indignation
On thee, the troubler of the poor world’s peace.
The worm of conscience still begnaw thy soul.
Thy friends suspect for traitors while thou liv’st,
And take deep traitors for thy dearest friends.
No sleep close up that deadly eye of thine,
Unless it be while some tormenting dream
Affrights thee with a hell of ugly devils.
Thou elvish-marked, abortive, rooting hog,
Thou was sealed in they nativity
The slave of nature and the son of hell,
Thou slander of they heavy mother's womb,
Thou loathed issue of they father's loins,
Thou rag of honor, thou detested—

sentences.speaker: RICHARD
sentences.text: Margaret.

sentences.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
sentences.text: Richard!

sentences.speaker: RICHARD
sentences.text: Ha?

sentences.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
sentences.text: I call thee not.

sentences.speaker: RICHARD
sentences.text:
I cry thee mercy, then, for I did think
That thou hadst called me all these bitter names.

sentences.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
sentences.text:
Why, so I did, but looked for no reply.
O, let me make the period to my curse!

sentences.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
sentences.text:
[to QUEEN MARGARET]
Thus have you breathed your curse against yourself.

sentences.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
sentences.text:
Poor painted queen, vain flourish of my fortune,
Why strew’st thou sugar on that bottled spider,
Whose deadly web ensnareth thee about?
Fool, fool, thou whet’st a knife to kill thyself.
The day will come that thou shalt wish for me
To help thee curse that poisonous bunch-backed toad.

sentences.speaker: HASTINGS
sentences.text:
False-boding woman, end thy frantic curse,
Lest to thy harm thou move our patience.

sentences.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
sentences.text: Foul shame upon you, you have all moved mine.

sentences.speaker: RIVERS
sentences.text: Were you well served, you would be taught your duty.

sentences.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
sentences.text:
To serve me well, you all should do me duty:
Teach me to be your queen, and you my subjects.
O, serve me well, and teach yourselves that duty!

sentences.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
sentences.text:

O, serve me well, and teach yourselves that duty!

sentences.speaker: DORSET
sentences.text: [to RIVERS] Dispute not with her; she is lunatic.

sentences.speaker: DORSET
sentences.text: [to RIVERS] Dispute not with her; she is lunatic.

sentences.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
sentences.text:
Peace, Master Marquess, you are malapert.
Your fire-new stamp of honor is scarce current.
O, that your young nobility could judge
What ’twere to lose it and be miserable!
They that stand high have many blasts to shake them,
And if they fall, they dash themselves to pieces.

sentences.speaker: RICHARD
sentences.text: Good counsel, marry. —Learn it, learn it, marquess.

sentences.speaker: DORSET
sentences.text: It touches you, my lord, as much as me.

sentences.speaker: RICHARD
sentences.text:
Ay, and much more; but I was born so high.
Our aerie buildeth in the cedar’s top,
And dallies with the wind and scorns the sun.

sentences.speaker: RICHARD
sentences.text:
Ay, and much more; but I was born so high.
Our aerie buildeth in the cedar’s top,
And dallies with the wind and scorns the sun.

sentences.speaker: RICHARD
sentences.text:
Ay, and much more; but I was born so high.
Our aerie buildeth in the cedar’s top,
And dallies with the wind and scorns the sun.

sentences.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
sentences.text:
And turns the sun to shade. Alas, alas,
Witness my son, now in the shade of death,
Whose bright out-shining beams thy cloudy wrath
Hath in eternal darkness folded up.
Your aerie buildeth in our aerie’s nest.
O God, that seest it, do not suffer it!
As it was won with blood, lost be it so.

sentences.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
sentences.text: Peace, peace, for shame, if not for charity.

sentences.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
sentences.text:
Urge neither charity nor shame to me.
[addressing the others]
Uncharitably with me have you dealt,
And shamefully my hopes by you are butchered.
My charity is outrage, life my shame,
And in that shame still live my sorrows' rage.

sentences.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET

sentences.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
sentences.text: Have done, have done.

sentences.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
sentences.text:
O princely Buckingham, I’ll kiss thy hand
In sign of league and amity with thee.
Now fair befall thee and thy noble house!
Thy garments are not spotted with our blood,
Nor thou within the compass of my curse.

sentences.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
sentences.text:
Nor no one here, for curses never pass
The lips of those that breathe them in the air.

sentences.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
sentences.text:
Nor no one here, for curses never pass
The lips of those that breathe them in the air.

sentences.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
sentences.text:
I will not think but they ascend the sky,
And there awake God’s gentle-sleeping peace.
[aside to BUCKINGHAM]
O Buckingham, take heed of yonder dog!
Look when he fawns, he bites; and when he bites,
His venom tooth will rankle to the death.
Have naught to do with him. Beware of him.
Sin, death, and hell have set their marks on him,
And all their ministers attend on him.

sentences.speaker: RICHARD
sentences.text: What doth she say, my lord of Buckingham?

sentences.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
sentences.text: Nothing that I respect, my gracious lord.

sentences.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
sentences.text:
What, dost thou scorn me for my gentle counsel,
And soothe the devil that I warn thee from?
O, but remember this another day,
When he shall split thy very heart with sorrow,
And say poor Margaret was a prophetess.—
Live each of you the subjects to his hate,
And he to yours, and all of you to God’s.

sentences.speaker: HASTINGS
sentences.text: My hair doth stand an end to hear her curses.

sentences.speaker: RIVERS
sentences.text: And so doth mine. I muse why she’s at liberty.

sentences.speaker: RIVERS
sentences.text: And so doth mine. I muse why she’s at liberty.

sentences.speaker: RICHARD
sentences.text:
I cannot blame her. By God’s holy mother,
She hath had too much wrong, and I repent
My part thereof that I have done to her.

sentences.speaker: RICHARD
sentences.text:

She hath had too much wrong, and I repent
My part thereof that I have done to her.

sentences.speaker: RICHARD

I cannot blame her. By God’s holy mother,
She hath had too much wrong, and I repent
My part thereof that I have done to her.

sentences.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
sentences.text: I never did her any, to my knowledge.

sentences.speaker: RICHARD
sentences.text:
Yet you have all the vantage of her wrong.
I was too hot to do somebody good
That is too cold in thinking of it now.
Marry, as for Clarence, he is well repaid;
He is franked up to fatting for his pains.
God pardon them that are the cause thereof.

sentences.speaker: RIVERS
sentences.text:
A virtuous and a Christian-like conclusion
To pray for them that have done scathe to us.

sentences.speaker: RICHARD
sentences.text:
So do I ever [aside] being well-advised,
For had I cursed now, I had cursed myself.
Enter CATESBY

sentences.speaker: CATESBY
sentences.text:
Madam, his majesty doth call for you,—
And for your Grace, —and yours, my gracious lords.

sentences.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
sentences.text: Catesby, I come. —Lords, will you go with me?

sentences.speaker: RIVERS
sentences.text: We wait upon your Grace.
Exeunt all but RICHARD, Duke of Gloucester

sentences.speaker: RICHARD
sentences.text:
I do the wrong, and first begin to brawl.
The secret mischiefs that I set abroach
I lay unto the grievous charge of others.
Clarence, whom I indeed have cast in darkness,
I do beweep to many simple gulls,
Namely, to Derby, Hastings, Buckingham,
And tell them ’tis the queen and her allies
That stir the king against the duke my brother.
Now they believe it and withal whet me
To be revenged on Rivers, Dorset, Grey;
But then I sigh and, with a piece of scripture,
Tell them that God bids us do good for evil;
And thus I clothe my naked villainy
With odd old ends stolen out of Holy Writ,
And seem a saint when most I play the devil.

Enter two MURDERERS

sentences.speaker: RICHARD
sentences.text:
But, soft! here come my executioners.—
How now, my hardy, stout, resolvèd mates?
Are you now going to dispatch this thing?

sentences.speaker: RICHARD
sentences.text:
But, soft! here come my executioners.—
How now, my hardy, stout, resolvèd mates?
Are you now going to dispatch this thing?

sentences.speaker: RICHARD
sentences.text:
But, soft! here come my executioners.—
How now, my hardy, stout, resolvèd mates?
Are you now going to dispatch this thing?

sentences.speaker: FIRST MURDERER
sentences.text:
We are, my lord, and come to have the warrant
That we may be admitted where he is.

sentences.speaker: FIRST MURDERER
sentences.text:
We are, my lord, and come to have the warrant
That we may be admitted where he is.

sentences.speaker: RICHARD
sentences.text:
Well thought upon. I have it here about me.
[He gives a paper]
When you have done, repair to Crosby Place.
But, sirs, be sudden in the execution,
Withal obdurate; do not hear him plead,
For Clarence is well-spoken and perhaps
May move your hearts to pity if you mark him.

sentences.speaker: FIRST MURDERER
sentences.text:
Tut, tut, my lord, we will not stand to prate.
Talkers are no good doers. Be assured
We go to use our hands and not our tongues.

sentences.speaker: FIRST MURDERER
sentences.text:

sentences.speaker: RICHARD
sentences.text:
Your eyes drop millstones, when fools' eyes drop tears.
I like you lads. About your business straight.
Go, go, dispatch.

sentences.speaker: MURDERERS
sentences.text: We will, my noble lord.
Exeunt
Enter CLARENCE and BRAKENBURY

sentences.speaker: BRAKENBURY
sentences.text: Why looks your grace so heavily today?

sentences.speaker: CLARENCE
sentences.text:
O, I have passed a miserable night,
So full of ugly dreams, of ugly sights,
That, as I am a Christian faithful man,
I would not spend another such a night
Though ’twere to buy a world of happy days,
So full of dismal terror was the time.

sentences.speaker: CLARENCE
sentences.text:
O, I have passed a miserable night,

That, as I am a Christian faithful man,
I would not spend another such a night
Though ’twere to buy a world of happy days,
So full of dismal terror was the time.

sentences.speaker: BRAKENBURY
sentences.text: What was your dream, my lord? I pray you tell me.

sentences.speaker: BRAKENBURY
sentences.text: What was your dream, my lord? I pray you tell me.

sentences.speaker: CLARENCE
sentences.text:
Methoughts that I had broken from the Tower
And was embarked to cross to Burgundy,
And in my company my brother Gloucester,
Who from my cabin tempted me to walk
Upon the hatches. Thence we looked toward England
And cited up a thousand fearful times,
During the wars of York and Lancaster
That had befall'n us. As we paced along
Upon the giddy footing of the hatches,
Methought that Gloucester stumbled, and in falling
Struck me, that thought to stay him, overboard
Into the tumbling billows of the main.
O Lord, methought what pain it was to drown,
What dreadful noise of waters in my ears,
What sights of ugly death within my eyes.
Methoughts I saw a thousand fearful wracks,
Ten thousand men that fishes gnawed upon,
Wedges of gold, great anchors, heaps of pearl,
Inestimable stones, unvalued jewels,
All scattered in the bottom of the sea.
Some lay in dead men's skulls, and in the holes
Where eyes did once inhabit, there were crept—
as 'twere in scorn of eyes— reflecting gems,
That wooed the slimy bottom of the deep
And mocked the dead bones that lay scattered by.

That wooed the slimy bottom of the deep
And mocked the dead bones that lay scattered by.

sentences.speaker: CLARENCE
sentences.text:
Methoughts that I had broken from the Tower
And was embarked to cross to Burgundy,
And in my company my brother Gloucester,
Who from my cabin tempted me to walk
Upon the hatches. Thence we looked toward England
And cited up a thousand fearful times,
During the wars of York and Lancaster
That had befall'n us. As we paced along
Upon the giddy footing of the hatches,
Methought that Gloucester stumbled, and in falling
Struck me, that thought to stay him, overboard
Into the tumbling billows of the main.
O Lord, methought what pain it was to drown,
What dreadful noise of waters in my ears,
What sights of ugly death within my eyes.
Methoughts I saw a thousand fearful wracks,
Ten thousand men that fishes gnawed upon,
Wedges of gold, great anchors, heaps of pearl,
Inestimable stones, unvalued jewels,
All scattered in the bottom of the sea.
Some lay in dead men's skulls, and in the holes
Where eyes did once inhabit, there were crept—
as 'twere in scorn of eyes— reflecting gems,
That wooed the slimy bottom of the deep
And mocked the dead bones that lay scattered by.

sentences.speaker: BRAKENBURY
sentences.text:
Had you such leisure in the time of death
To gaze upon the secrets of the deep?

sentences.speaker: CLARENCE
sentences.text:
Methought I had, and often did I strive
To yield the ghost, but still the envious flood
Stopped in my soul and would not let it forth
To find the empty, vast, and wand'ring air,
But smothered it within my panting bulk,
Who almost burst to belch it in the sea.

sentences.speaker: BRAKENBURY
sentences.text: Awaked you not in this sore agony?

sentences.speaker: CLARENCE
sentences.text:
No, no, my dream was lengthened after life.
O, then began the tempest to my soul.
I passed, methought, the melancholy flood,
With that sour ferryman which poets write of,
Unto the kingdom of perpetual night.
The first that there did greet my stranger-soul
Was my great father-in-law, renownèd Warwick,
Who cried aloud, “What scourge for perjury
Can this dark monarchy afford false Clarence?”
And so he vanished. Then came wand'ring by
A shadow like an angel, with bright hair
Dabbled in blood, and he shrieked out aloud
"Clarence is come—false, fleeting, perjured Clarence,
That stabbed me in the field by Tewkesbury.
Seize on him, furies. Take him unto torment."
With that, methoughts, a legion of foul fiends
Environed me and howled in mine ears
Such hideous cries that with the very noise
I trembling waked, and for a season after
Could not believe but that I was in hell,
Such terrible impression made my dream.

sentences.speaker: BRAKENBURY
sentences.text:
No marvel, lord, though it affrighted you.
I am afraid, methinks, to hear you tell it.

sentences.speaker: BRAKENBURY
sentences.text:
No marvel, lord, though it affrighted you.
I am afraid, methinks, to hear you tell it.

sentences.speaker: CLARENCE
sentences.text:
Ah keeper, keeper, I have done those things,
That now give evidence against my soul,
For Edward’s sake, and see how he requites me.—
O God, if my deep prayers cannot appease thee,
But thou wilt be avenged on my misdeeds,
Yet execute thy wrath in me alone!
O, spare my guiltless wife and my poor children!—
Keeper, I prithee sit by me awhile.
My soul is heavy, and I fain would sleep.

sentences.speaker: BRAKENBURY
sentences.text: I will, my lord. God give your Grace good rest.

sentences.speaker: BRAKENBURY
sentences.text: I will, my lord. God give your Grace good rest.
CLARENCE sleeps

sentences.speaker: BRAKENBURY
sentences.text:
Sorrow breaks seasons and reposing hours,
Makes the night morning, and the noontide night.
Princes have but their titles for their glories,
An outward honor for an inward toil,
And, for unfelt imaginations,
They often feel a world of restless cares,
So that betwixt their titles and low name
There's nothing differs but the outward fame.

Enter the two MURDERERS

sentences.speaker: FIRST MURDERER
sentences.text: Ho, who’s here?

sentences.speaker: BRAKENBURY
sentences.text: What wouldst thou, fellow? And how cam’st thou hither?

sentences.speaker: BRAKENBURY
sentences.text: What wouldst thou, fellow? And how cam’st thou hither?

sentences.speaker: SECOND MURDERER
sentences.text:
I would speak with Clarence, and I came hither on my
legs.

sentences.speaker: BRAKENBURY
sentences.text: What, so brief?

sentences.speaker: FIRST MURDERER
sentences.text:
'Tis better, sir, to be brief than tedious.—Let him see
our commission, and talk no more.

sentences.speaker: FIRST MURDERER
sentences.text:
'Tis better, sir, to be brief than tedious.—Let him see
our commission, and talk no more.

sentences.speaker: FIRST MURDERER
sentences.text:
'Tis better, sir, to be brief than tedious.—Let him see
our commission, and talk no more.

sentences.speaker: BRAKENBURY
sentences.text:
I am in this commanded to deliver
The noble duke of Clarence to your hands.
I will not reason what is meant hereby
Because I will be guiltless from the meaning.
There lies the duke asleep, and there the keys.
[He hands them the keys]
I’ll to the king and signify to him
That thus I have resigned my charge to you.

sentences.speaker: FIRST MURDERER
sentences.text: You may, sir. 'Tis a point of wisdom. Fare you well.

sentences.speaker: FIRST MURDERER
sentences.text: You may, sir. 'Tis a point of wisdom. Fare you well.

sentences.speaker: FIRST MURDERER

Exit BRAKENBURY
What, shall I stab him as he sleeps?

sentences.speaker: FIRST MURDERER
sentences.text: No. He’ll say ’twas done cowardly, when he wakes.

sentences.speaker: FIRST MURDERER
sentences.text: No. He’ll say ’twas done cowardly, when he wakes.

sentences.speaker: SECOND MURDERER
sentences.text: Why, he shall never wake until the great Judgment Day.

sentences.speaker: FIRST MURDERER
sentences.text: Why, then he’ll say we stabbed him sleeping.

sentences.speaker: SECOND MURDERER
sentences.text:
The urging of that word “judgment” hath bred a kind of
remorse in me.

sentences.speaker: FIRST MURDERER
sentences.text: What, art thou afraid?

sentences.speaker: SECOND MURDERER
sentences.text:
Not to kill him, having a warrant, but to be damned
for killing him, from the which no warrant can defend
me.

sentences.speaker: SECOND MURDERER
sentences.text:
Not to kill him, having a warrant, but to be damned
for killing him, from the which no warrant can defend
me.

sentences.speaker: SECOND MURDERER

Not to kill him, having a warrant, but to be damned
for killing him, from the which no warrant can defend
me.

sentences.speaker: FIRST MURDERER
sentences.text: I thought thou hadst been resolute.

sentences.speaker: SECOND MURDERER
sentences.text: So I am—to let him live.

sentences.speaker: FIRST MURDERER
sentences.text: I’ll back to the duke of Gloucester and tell him so.

sentences.speaker: SECOND MURDERER
sentences.text:
Nay, I prithee stay a little. I hope this passionate
humor of mine will change. It was wont to hold me but
while one tells twenty.

sentences.speaker: SECOND MURDERER
sentences.text:
Nay, I prithee stay a little. I hope this passionate
humor of mine will change. It was wont to hold me but
while one tells twenty.

sentences.speaker: SECOND MURDERER
sentences.text:
Nay, I prithee stay a little. I hope this passionate
humor of mine will change. It was wont to hold me but
while one tells twenty.

sentences.speaker: FIRST MURDERER
sentences.text: How dost thou feel thyself now?

sentences.speaker: SECOND MURDERER
sentences.text:
Faith, some certain dregs of conscience are yet within
me.

sentences.speaker: FIRST MURDERER
sentences.text: Remember our reward when the deed’s done.

sentences.speaker: SECOND MURDERER
sentences.text: Zounds, he dies! I had forgot the reward.

sentences.speaker: SECOND MURDERER
sentences.text: Zounds, he dies! I had forgot the reward.

sentences.speaker: FIRST MURDERER
sentences.text: Where’s thy conscience now?

sentences.speaker: SECOND MURDERER
sentences.text: O, in the duke of Gloucester’s purse.

sentences.speaker: FIRST MURDERER
sentences.text:
So when he opens his purse to give us our reward, thy
conscience flies out.

sentences.speaker: SECOND MURDERER
sentences.text:
'Tis no matter. Let it go. There’s few or none will
entertain it.

sentences.speaker: SECOND MURDERER
sentences.text:
'Tis no matter. Let it go. There’s few or none will
entertain it.

sentences.speaker: SECOND MURDERER
sentences.text:
'Tis no matter. Let it go. There’s few or none will
entertain it.

sentences.speaker: FIRST MURDERER
sentences.text: What if it come to thee again?

sentences.speaker: SECOND MURDERER
sentences.text:
I’ll not meddle with it. It makes a man a coward: a man
cannot steal but it accuseth him; a man cannot swear
but it checks him; a man cannot lie with his neighbor’s
wife but it detects him. 'Tis a blushing, shamefaced
spirit that mutinies in a man’s bosom. It fills a man
full of obstacles. It made me once restore a purse of
gold that by chance I found. It beggars any man that
keeps it. It is turned out of towns and cities for a
dangerous thing, and every man that means to live well
endeavors to trust to himself and live without it.

full of obstacles. It made me once restore a purse of
gold that by chance I found. It beggars any man that
keeps it. It is turned out of towns and cities for a
dangerous thing, and every man that means to live well
endeavors to trust to himself and live without it.

sentences.speaker: SECOND MURDERER
sentences.text:
I’ll not meddle with it. It makes a man a coward: a man
cannot steal but it accuseth him; a man cannot swear
but it checks him; a man cannot lie with his neighbor’s
wife but it detects him. 'Tis a blushing, shamefaced
spirit that mutinies in a man’s bosom. It fills a man
full of obstacles. It made me once restore a purse of
gold that by chance I found. It beggars any man that
keeps it. It is turned out of towns and cities for a
dangerous thing, and every man that means to live well
endeavors to trust to himself and live without it.

sentences.speaker: FIRST MURDERER
sentences.text:
Zounds, ’tis even now at my elbow, persuading me not to
kill the duke.

sentences.speaker: SECOND MURDERER
sentences.text:
Take the devil in thy mind, and believe him not. He
would insinuate with thee but to make thee sigh.

sentences.speaker: SECOND MURDERER
sentences.text:
Take the devil in thy mind, and believe him not. He
would insinuate with thee but to make thee sigh.

sentences.speaker: FIRST MURDERER
sentences.text: I am strong-framed. He cannot prevail with me.

sentences.speaker: FIRST MURDERER
sentences.text: I am strong-framed. He cannot prevail with me.

sentences.speaker: SECOND MURDERER
sentences.text:
Spoke like a tall man that respects thy reputation.
Come, shall we fall to work?

sentences.speaker: SECOND MURDERER
sentences.text:
Spoke like a tall man that respects thy reputation.
Come, shall we fall to work?

sentences.speaker: FIRST MURDERER
sentences.text:
Take him on the costard with the hilts of thy sword,
and then throw him into the malmsey butt in the next
room.

sentences.speaker: SECOND MURDERER
sentences.text: O excellent device— and make a sop of him.

sentences.speaker: FIRST MURDERER
sentences.text: Soft, he wakes.

sentences.speaker: SECOND MURDERER
sentences.text: Strike!

sentences.speaker: FIRST MURDERER
sentences.text: No, we’ll reason with him.

sentences.speaker: FIRST MURDERER
sentences.text: No, we’ll reason with him.

sentences.speaker: CLARENCE
sentences.text: Where art thou, keeper? Give me a cup of wine.

sentences.speaker: CLARENCE
sentences.text: Where art thou, keeper? Give me a cup of wine.

sentences.speaker: SECOND MURDERER
sentences.text: You shall have wine enough, my lord, anon.

sentences.speaker: CLARENCE
sentences.text: In God’s name, what art thou?

sentences.speaker: FIRST MURDERER
sentences.text: A man, as you are.

sentences.speaker: CLARENCE
sentences.text: But not, as I am, royal.

sentences.speaker: FIRST MURDERER
sentences.text: Nor you, as we are, loyal.

sentences.speaker: CLARENCE
sentences.text: Thy voice is thunder, but thy looks are humble.

sentences.speaker: FIRST MURDERER
sentences.text: My voice is now the king’s, my looks mine own.

sentences.speaker: CLARENCE
sentences.text:
How darkly and how deadly dost thou speak!
Your eyes do menace me. Why look you pale?
Who sent you hither? Wherefore do you come?

sentences.speaker: CLARENCE
sentences.text:
How darkly and how deadly dost thou speak!
Your eyes do menace me. Why look you pale?
Who sent you hither? Wherefore do you come?

sentences.speaker: CLARENCE
sentences.text:

sentences.text:

sentences.speaker: CLARENCE
sentences.text:
How darkly and how deadly dost thou speak!
Your eyes do menace me. Why look you pale?
Who sent you hither? Wherefore do you come?

sentences.speaker: SECOND MURDERER
sentences.text: To, to, to—

sentences.speaker: CLARENCE
sentences.text: To murder me?

sentences.speaker: BOTH MURDERERS
sentences.text: Ay, ay.

sentences.speaker: CLARENCE
sentences.text:
You scarcely have the hearts to tell me so,
And therefore cannot have the hearts to do it.
Wherein, my friends, have I offended you?

sentences.speaker: CLARENCE
sentences.text:
You scarcely have the hearts to tell me so,
And therefore cannot have the hearts to do it.
Wherein, my friends, have I offended you?

sentences.speaker: FIRST MURDERER
sentences.text: Offended us you have not, but the king.

sentences.speaker: CLARENCE
sentences.text: I shall be reconciled to him again.

sentences.speaker: SECOND MURDERER
sentences.text: Never, my lord. Therefore prepare to die.

sentences.speaker: SECOND MURDERER
sentences.text: Never, my lord. Therefore prepare to die.

sentences.speaker: CLARENCE
sentences.text:
Are you drawn forth among a world of men
To slay the innocent? What is my offense?
Where is the evidence that doth accuse me?
What lawful quest have given their verdict up
Unto the frowning judge? Or who pronounced
The bitter sentence of poor Clarence' death
Before I be convict by course of law?
To threaten me with death is most unlawful.
I charge you, as you hope to have redemption,
By Christ’s dear blood shed for our grievous sins,
That you depart, and lay no hands on me.
The deed you undertake is damnable.

sentences.speaker: FIRST MURDERER
sentences.text: What we will do, we do upon command.

sentences.speaker: SECOND MURDERER
sentences.text: And he that hath commanded is our king.

sentences.speaker: CLARENCE
sentences.text:
Erroneous vassals, the great King of kings
Hath in the tables of His law commanded
That thou shalt do no murder. Will thou then
Spurn at His edict and fulfill a man’s?
Take heed, for He holds vengeance in His hand
To hurl upon their heads that break His law.

sentences.speaker: CLARENCE
sentences.text:
Erroneous vassals, the great King of kings
Hath in the tables of His law commanded
That thou shalt do no murder. Will thou then
Spurn at His edict and fulfill a man’s?
Take heed, for He holds vengeance in His hand
To hurl upon their heads that break His law.

sentences.speaker: SECOND MURDERER
sentences.text:
And that same vengeance doth He hurl on thee
For false forswearing and for murder too.
Thou didst receive the sacrament to fight
In quarrel of the house of Lancaster.

sentences.speaker: SECOND MURDERER
sentences.text:
And that same vengeance doth He hurl on thee
For false forswearing and for murder too.
Thou didst receive the sacrament to fight
In quarrel of the house of Lancaster.

sentences.speaker: FIRST MURDERER
sentences.text:
And, like a traitor to the name of God,
Didst break that vow, and with thy treacherous blade
Unrippedst the bowels of thy sovereign’s son.

sentences.speaker: SECOND MURDERER
sentences.text: Whom thou wert sworn to cherish and defend.

sentences.speaker: FIRST MURDERER
sentences.text:
How canst thou urge God’s dreadful law to us
When thou hast broke it in such dear degree?

sentences.speaker: CLARENCE
sentences.text:
Alas! For whose sake did I that ill deed?
For Edward, for my brother, for his sake.
He sends you not to murder me for this,
For in that sin he is as deep as I.
If God will be avengèd for this deed,
O, know you yet He doth it publicly!
Take not the quarrel from His powerful arm;
He needs no indirect or lawless course
To cut off those that have offended Him.

sentences.text:

sentences.speaker: FIRST MURDERER
sentences.text:
Who made thee then a bloody minister
When gallant-springing, brave Plantagenet,
That princely novice, was struck dead by thee?

sentences.speaker: CLARENCE
sentences.text: My brother’s love, the devil, and my rage.

sentences.speaker: FIRST MURDERER
sentences.text:
Thy brother’s love, our duty, and thy faults
Provoke us hither now to slaughter thee.

sentences.speaker: CLARENCE
sentences.text:
If you do love my brother, hate not me.
I am his brother, and I love him well.
If you are hired for meed, go back again,
And I will send you to my brother Gloucester,
Who shall reward you better for my life
Than Edward will for tidings of my death.

sentences.speaker: CLARENCE
sentences.text:

If you are hired for meed, go back again,
And I will send you to my brother Gloucester,
Who shall reward you better for my life
Than Edward will for tidings of my death.

sentences.speaker: SECOND MURDERER
sentences.text: You are deceived. Your brother Gloucester hates you.

sentences.speaker: SECOND MURDERER
sentences.text: You are deceived. Your brother Gloucester hates you.

sentences.speaker: CLARENCE
sentences.text:
O, no, he loves me, and he holds me dear.
Go you to him from me.

sentences.speaker: CLARENCE
sentences.text:
O, no, he loves me, and he holds me dear.
Go you to him from me.

sentences.speaker: FIRST MURDERER
sentences.text: Ay, so we will.

sentences.speaker: CLARENCE
sentences.text:
Tell him, when that our princely father York
Blessed his three sons with his victorious arm,
And charged us from his soul to love each other,
He little thought of this divided friendship.
Bid Gloucester think of this, and he will weep.

sentences.speaker: CLARENCE
sentences.text:
Tell him, when that our princely father York
Blessed his three sons with his victorious arm,
And charged us from his soul to love each other,
He little thought of this divided friendship.
Bid Gloucester think of this, and he will weep.

sentences.speaker: FIRST MURDERER
sentences.text: Ay, millstones, as he lessoned us to weep.

sentences.speaker: CLARENCE
sentences.text: O, do not slander him, for he is kind.

sentences.speaker: FIRST MURDERER
sentences.text:
As snow in harvest. Come, you deceive yourself.
'Tis he that sends us to destroy you here.

sentences.speaker: FIRST MURDERER
sentences.text:
As snow in harvest. Come, you deceive yourself.
'Tis he that sends us to destroy you here.

sentences.speaker: FIRST MURDERER
sentences.text:
As snow in harvest. Come, you deceive yourself.
'Tis he that sends us to destroy you here.

sentences.speaker: CLARENCE
sentences.text:
It cannot be, for he bewept my fortune,
And hugged me in his arms, and swore with sobs
That he would labor my delivery.

sentences.speaker: CLARENCE
sentences.text:
It cannot be, for he bewept my fortune,
And hugged me in his arms, and swore with sobs
That he would labor my delivery.

sentences.speaker: CLARENCE

It cannot be, for he bewept my fortune,
And hugged me in his arms, and swore with sobs
That he would labor my delivery.

sentences.speaker: SECOND MURDERER
sentences.text:
Why, so he doth, when he delivers you
From this earth’s thralldom to the joys of heaven.

sentences.speaker: FIRST MURDERER
sentences.text: Make peace with God, for you must die, my lord.

sentences.speaker: CLARENCE
sentences.text:
Have you that holy feeling in your souls
To counsel me to make my peace with God,
And art you yet to your own souls so blind
That thou will war with God by murd'ring me?
O sirs, consider: they that set you on
To do this deed will hate you for the deed.

sentences.speaker: CLARENCE

O sirs, consider: they that set you on
To do this deed will hate you for the deed.

O sirs, consider: they that set you on

sentences.speaker: SECOND MURDERER
sentences.text: [to FIRST MURDERER] What shall we do?

sentences.speaker: CLARENCE
sentences.text:
Relent, and save your souls.
Which of you—if you were a prince's son
Being pent from liberty, as I am now—
If two such murderers as yourselves came to you,
Would you not entreat for life? Ay, you would beg,
Were you in my distress.

sentences.speaker: CLARENCE
sentences.text:

Being pent from liberty, as I am now—
If two such murderers as yourselves came to you,
Would you not entreat for life? Ay, you would beg,
Were you in my distress.

sentences.speaker: FIRST MURDERER
sentences.text: Relent? No. 'Tis cowardly and womanish.

sentences.speaker: FIRST MURDERER
sentences.text: Relent? No. 'Tis cowardly and womanish.

sentences.speaker: FIRST MURDERER
sentences.text: Relent? No. 'Tis cowardly and womanish.

sentences.speaker: CLARENCE
sentences.text:
Not to relent is beastly, savage, devilish.
[to SECOND MURDERER]
My friend, I spy some pity in thy looks.
O, if thine eye be not a flatterer,
Come thou on my side and entreat for me.
A begging prince what beggar pities not?

sentences.speaker: SECOND MURDERER
sentences.text: Look behind you, my lord.

sentences.speaker: FIRST MURDERER
sentences.text:
Take that, and that. [stabs CLARENCE]
If all this will not do,
I’ll drown you in the malmsey butt within.
Exit with the body

sentences.speaker: SECOND MURDERER
sentences.text:
A bloody deed, and desperately dispatched.
How fain, like Pilate, would I wash my hands
Of this most grievous murder.

sentences.speaker: SECOND MURDERER
sentences.text:

Enter FIRST MURDERER

sentences.speaker: FIRST MURDERER
sentences.text:
How now? What mean’st thou, that thou help’st me not?
By heavens, the duke shall know how slack you have
been.

sentences.speaker: FIRST MURDERER
sentences.text:

By heavens, the duke shall know how slack you have
been.

sentences.speaker: FIRST MURDERER

been.

sentences.speaker: SECOND MURDERER
sentences.text:
I would he knew that I had saved his brother.
Take thou the fee, and tell him what I say,
For I repent me that the duke is slain.

sentences.speaker: SECOND MURDERER

For I repent me that the duke is slain.

sentences.speaker: FIRST MURDERER
sentences.text:
So do not I. Go, coward as thou art.
Well, I’ll go hide the body in some hole
Till that the duke give order for his burial.
And when I have my meed, I will away,
For this will out, and then I must not stay.

sentences.speaker: KING EDWARD
sentences.text:
Why, so. Now have I done a good day’s work.
You peers, continue this united league.
I every day expect an embassage
From my Redeemer to redeem me hence,
And more in peace my soul shall part to heaven
Since I have made my friends at peace on earth
Rivers and Hastings, take each other’s hand.
Dissemble not your hatred. Swear your love.

sentences.speaker: RIVERS
RIVERS
HASTINGS
sentences.text: So thrive I as I truly swear the like.

sentences.speaker: KING EDWARD
sentences.text:
Take heed you dally not before your king,
Lest He that is the supreme King of kings
Confound your hidden falsehood, and award
Either of you to be the other’s end.

sentences.speaker: KING EDWARD
sentences.text:
Take heed you dally not before your king,
Lest He that is the supreme King of kings
Confound your hidden falsehood, and award
Either of you to be the other’s end.

sentences.speaker: HASTINGS
sentences.text: So prosper I as I swear perfect love.

sentences.speaker: RIVERS
sentences.text: And I as I love Hastings with my heart.

sentences.speaker: KING EDWARD
sentences.text:
[to QUEEN ELIZABETH]
Madam, yourself is not exempt in this,—
Nor you, son Dorset, —Buckingham, nor you.
You have been factious one against the other.—
Wife, love Lord Hastings. Let him kiss your hand,
And what you do, do it unfeignedly.

sentences.speaker: KING EDWARD
sentences.text:

sentences.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
sentences.text:
There, Hastings, I will never more remember
Our former hatred, so thrive I and mine.

sentences.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
sentences.text:
There, Hastings, I will never more remember
Our former hatred, so thrive I and mine.
HASTINGS kisses her hand

sentences.speaker: KING EDWARD
sentences.text: Dorset, embrace him.—Hastings, love Lord Marquess.

sentences.speaker: KING EDWARD
sentences.text: Dorset, embrace him.—Hastings, love Lord Marquess.

sentences.speaker: DORSET
sentences.text:
This interchange of love, I here protest,
Upon my part shall be inviolable.

sentences.speaker: HASTINGS
sentences.text: And so swear I.
They embrace

sentences.speaker: KING EDWARD
sentences.text:
Now, princely Buckingham, seal thou this league
With thy embracements to my wife’s allies
And make me happy in your unity.

sentences.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
sentences.text:
[to QUEEN ELIZABETH]
Whenever Buckingham doth turn his hate
Upon your Grace, but with all duteous love
Doth cherish you and yours, God punish me
With hate in those where I expect most love.
When I have most need to employ a friend,
And most assurèd that he is a friend,
Deep, hollow, treacherous, and full of guile
Be he unto me: this do I beg of God
When I am cold in love to you or yours.

sentences.speaker: KING EDWARD
sentences.text:
A pleasing cordial, princely Buckingham,
Is this thy vow unto my sickly heart.
There wanteth now our brother Gloucester here
To make the blessèd period of this peace.

sentences.speaker: KING EDWARD
sentences.text:
A pleasing cordial, princely Buckingham,
Is this thy vow unto my sickly heart.
There wanteth now our brother Gloucester here
To make the blessèd period of this peace.

sentences.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
sentences.text:
And in good time,
Here comes Sir Richard Ratcliffe and the duke.
Enter RICHARD and RATCLIFFE

sentences.speaker: RICHARD
sentences.text:
Good morrow to my sovereign king and queen,
And, princely peers, a happy time of day.

sentences.speaker: RICHARD
sentences.text:
Good morrow to my sovereign king and queen,
And, princely peers, a happy time of day.

sentences.speaker: KING EDWARD
sentences.text:
Happy indeed, as we have spent the day.
Brother, we have done deeds of charity,
Made peace of enmity, fair love of hate,
Between these swelling, wrong-incensèd peers.

sentences.speaker: KING EDWARD
sentences.text:
Happy indeed, as we have spent the day.
Brother, we have done deeds of charity,
Made peace of enmity, fair love of hate,
Between these swelling, wrong-incensèd peers.

sentences.speaker: RICHARD
sentences.text:
A blessèd labor, my most sovereign lord.
Amongst this princely heap, if any here
By false intelligence, or wrong surmise
Hold me a foe,
If I unwittingly, or in my rage,
Have aught committed that is hardly borne
By any in this presence, I desire
To reconcile me to his friendly peace.
'Tis death to me to be at enmity;
I hate it, and desire all good men’s love.
First, madam, I entreat true peace of you,
Which I will purchase with my duteous service;—
Of you, my noble cousin Buckingham,
If ever any grudge were lodged between us;—
Of you, Lord Rivers, and Lord Gray of you,
Dukes, earls, lords, gentlemen; indeed of all!
I do not know that Englishman alive
With whom my soul is any jot at odds
More than the infant that is born tonight.
I thank my God for my humility.

sentences.text:
A holy day shall this be kept hereafter.
I would to God all strifes were well compounded.
My sovereign lord, I do beseech your Highness
To take our brother Clarence to your grace.

sentences.speaker: RICHARD
sentences.text:
Why, madam, have I offered love for this,
To be so flouted in this royal presence?
Who knows not that the gentle duke is dead?

sentences.speaker: RICHARD
sentences.text:

To be so flouted in this royal presence?
Who knows not that the gentle duke is dead?

sentences.text:

Who knows not that the gentle duke is dead?

sentences.speaker: RICHARD
sentences.text:
Why, madam, have I offered love for this,
To be so flouted in this royal presence?
Who knows not that the gentle duke is dead?

sentences.speaker: KING EDWARD
sentences.text: Who knows not he is dead! Who knows he is?

sentences.speaker: KING EDWARD
sentences.text: Who knows not he is dead! Who knows he is?

sentences.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
sentences.text: All-seeing heaven, what a world is this!

sentences.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
sentences.text: Look I so pale, Lord Dorset, as the rest?

sentences.speaker: DORSET
sentences.text:
Ay, my good lord, and no one in the presence
But his red color hath forsook his cheeks.

sentences.speaker: DORSET
sentences.text:
Ay, my good lord, and no one in the presence
But his red color hath forsook his cheeks.

sentences.speaker: KING EDWARD
sentences.text: Is Clarence dead? The order was reversed.

sentences.speaker: KING EDWARD
sentences.text: Is Clarence dead? The order was reversed.

sentences.speaker: RICHARD
sentences.text:
But he, poor man, by your first order died,
And that a wingèd Mercury did bear.
Some tardy cripple bear the countermand,
That came too lag to see him burièd.
God grant that some, less noble and less loyal,
Nearer in bloody thoughts, and not in blood,
Deserve not worse than wretched Clarence did,
And yet go current from suspicion.

sentences.speaker: RICHARD
sentences.text:

Enter Lord STANLEY, Earl of Derby

sentences.speaker: STANLEY
sentences.text: [kneeling] A boon, my sovereign, for my service done.

sentences.speaker: KING EDWARD
sentences.text: I prithee, peace. My soul is full of sorrow.

sentences.speaker: KING EDWARD
sentences.text: I prithee, peace. My soul is full of sorrow.

sentences.speaker: STANLEY
sentences.text: I will not rise unless your Highness hear me.

sentences.speaker: KING EDWARD
sentences.text: Then say at once what is it thou requests.

sentences.speaker: STANLEY
sentences.text:
The forfeit, sovereign, of my servant’s life,
Who slew today a riotous gentleman
Lately attendant on the duke of Norfolk.

sentences.speaker: KING EDWARD
sentences.text:
Have I a tongue to doom my brother’s death,
And shall the tongue give pardon to a slave?
My brother killed no man; his fault was thought,
And yet his punishment was bitter death.
Who sued to me for him? Who, in my wrath,
Kneeled at my feet, and bade me be advised?
Who spoke of brotherhood? Who spoke of love?
Who told me how the poor soul did forsake
The mighty Warwick and did fight for me?
Who told me, in the field by Tewkesbury,
When Oxford had me down, he rescued me,
And said “Dear brother, live, and be a king?”
Who told me, when we both lay in the field
Frozen almost to death, how he did lap me
Even in his garments and did give himself,
All thin and naked, to the numb-cold night?
All this from my remembrance brutish wrath
Sinfully plucked, and not a man of you
Had so much grace to put it in my mind.
But when your carters or your waiting vassals
Have done a drunken slaughter and defaced
The precious image of our dear Redeemer,
You straight are on your knees for pardon, pardon,
And I, unjustly too, must grant it you.
[STANLEY rises]
But for my brother, not a man would speak,
Nor I, ungracious, speak unto myself
For him, poor soul. The proudest of you all
Have been beholding to him in his life,
Yet none of you would once beg for his life.
O God, I fear Thy justice will take hold
On me and you, and mine and yours for this!—
Come, Hastings, help me to my closet.—
Ah, poor Clarence.

O God, I fear Thy justice will take hold
On me and you, and mine and yours for this!—
Come, Hastings, help me to my closet.—
Ah, poor Clarence.

sentences.speaker: KING EDWARD
sentences.text:
Have I a tongue to doom my brother’s death,
And shall the tongue give pardon to a slave?
My brother killed no man; his fault was thought,
And yet his punishment was bitter death.
Who sued to me for him? Who, in my wrath,
Kneeled at my feet, and bade me be advised?
Who spoke of brotherhood? Who spoke of love?
Who told me how the poor soul did forsake
The mighty Warwick and did fight for me?
Who told me, in the field by Tewkesbury,
When Oxford had me down, he rescued me,
And said “Dear brother, live, and be a king?”
Who told me, when we both lay in the field
Frozen almost to death, how he did lap me
Even in his garments and did give himself,
All thin and naked, to the numb-cold night?
All this from my remembrance brutish wrath
Sinfully plucked, and not a man of you
Had so much grace to put it in my mind.
But when your carters or your waiting vassals
Have done a drunken slaughter and defaced
The precious image of our dear Redeemer,
You straight are on your knees for pardon, pardon,
And I, unjustly too, must grant it you.
[STANLEY rises]
But for my brother, not a man would speak,
Nor I, ungracious, speak unto myself
For him, poor soul. The proudest of you all
Have been beholding to him in his life,
Yet none of you would once beg for his life.
O God, I fear Thy justice will take hold
On me and you, and mine and yours for this!—
Come, Hastings, help me to my closet.—
Ah, poor Clarence.

sentences.speaker: RICHARD
sentences.text:
This is the fruits of rashness. Marked you not
How that the guilty kindred of the queen
Looked pale when they did hear of Clarence' death?
O, they did urge it still unto the king.
God will revenge it. Come, lords, will you go
To comfort Edward with our company?

sentences.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
sentences.text: We wait upon your Grace.
Exeunt
Enter the old DUCHESS of York, with the two children of Clarence

sentences.speaker: BOY
sentences.text: Good grandam, tell us, is our father dead?

sentences.speaker: DUCHESS
sentences.text: No, boy.

sentences.speaker: GIRL
sentences.text:
Why do you weep so oft, and beat your breast,
And cry, “O Clarence, my unhappy son?”

sentences.speaker: BOY
sentences.text:
Why do you look on us and shake your head,
And call us orphans, wretches, castaways,
If that our noble father were alive?

sentences.speaker: DUCHESS
sentences.text:
My pretty cousins, you mistake me both.
I do lament the sickness of the king,
As loath to lose him, not your father’s death.
It were lost sorrow to wail one that’s lost.

sentences.speaker: DUCHESS
sentences.text:

sentences.speaker: BOY
sentences.text:
Then, you conclude, my grandam, he is dead.
The king mine uncle is to blame for it.
God will revenge it, whom I will importune
With earnest prayers, all to that effect.

sentences.speaker: GIRL
sentences.text: And so will I.

sentences.speaker: DUCHESS
sentences.text:
Peace, children, peace. The king doth love you well.
Incapable and shallow innocents,
You cannot guess who caused your father’s death.

sentences.speaker: DUCHESS

You cannot guess who caused your father’s death.

sentences.speaker: BOY
sentences.text:
Grandam, we can, for my good uncle Gloucester
Told me the king, provoked to it by the queen,
Devised impeachments to imprison him;
And when my uncle told me so, he wept,
And pitied me, and kindly kissed my cheek,
Bade me rely on him as on my father,
And he would love me dearly as a child.

sentences.speaker: DUCHESS
sentences.text:
Ah, that deceit should steal such gentle shape,
And with a virtuous visor hide deep vice.
He is my son, ay, and therein my shame,
Yet from my dugs he drew not this deceit.

sentences.speaker: DUCHESS

sentences.speaker: BOY
sentences.text: I cannot think it. Hark, what noise is this?

sentences.speaker: BOY
sentences.text: I cannot think it. Hark, what noise is this?
Enter QUEEN ELIZABETH, with her hair about her ears, RIVERS, and DORSET after her

sentences.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
sentences.text:
Ah, who shall hinder me to wail and weep,
To chide my fortune and torment myself?
I’ll join with black despair against my soul
And to myself become an enemy.

sentences.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
sentences.text:
Ah, who shall hinder me to wail and weep,
To chide my fortune and torment myself?
I’ll join with black despair against my soul
And to myself become an enemy.

sentences.speaker: DUCHESS
sentences.text: What means this scene of rude impatience?

sentences.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
sentences.text:
To make an act of tragic violence.
Edward, my lord, thy son, our king, is dead.
Why grow the branches when the root is gone?
Why wither not the leaves that want their sap?
If you will live, lament. If die, be brief,
That our swift-wingèd souls may catch the king’s,
Or, like obedient subjects, follow him
To his new kingdom of ne'er-changing night.

sentences.speaker: DUCHESS
sentences.text:
Ah, so much interest have I in thy sorrow
As I had title in thy noble husband.
I have bewept a worthy husband’s death
And lived with looking on his images;
But now two mirrors of his princely semblance
Are cracked in pieces by malignant death,
And I, for comfort, have but one false glass
That grieves me when I see my shame in him.
Thou art a widow, yet thou art a mother,
And hast the comfort of thy children left,
But death hath snatched my husband from mine arms
And plucked two crutches from my feeble hands,
Clarence and Edward. O, what cause have I,
Thine being but a moiety of my moan,
To overgo thy woes and drown thy cries!

sentences.speaker: BOY
sentences.text:
[to QUEEN ELIZABETH] Ah, aunt, you wept not for
our father’s death.
How can we aid you with our kindred tears?

sentences.speaker: BOY
sentences.text:
[to QUEEN ELIZABETH] Ah, aunt, you wept not for
our father’s death.
How can we aid you with our kindred tears?

sentences.speaker: GIRL
sentences.text:
Our fatherless distress was left unmoaned.
Your widow-dolor likewise be unwept!

sentences.speaker: GIRL
sentences.text:
Our fatherless distress was left unmoaned.
Your widow-dolor likewise be unwept!

sentences.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
sentences.text:
G ive me no help in lamentation.
I am not barren to bring forth complaints.
All springs reduce their currents to mine eyes,
That I, being governed by the watery moon,
May send forth plenteous tears to drown the world.
Ah, for my husband, for my dear lord Edward!

That I, being governed by the watery moon,
May send forth plenteous tears to drown the world.
Ah, for my husband, for my dear lord Edward!

sentences.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
sentences.text:
G ive me no help in lamentation.
I am not barren to bring forth complaints.
All springs reduce their currents to mine eyes,
That I, being governed by the watery moon,
May send forth plenteous tears to drown the world.
Ah, for my husband, for my dear lord Edward!

sentences.speaker: CHILDREN
sentences.text: Ah, for our father, for our dear lord Clarence!

sentences.speaker: DUCHESS
sentences.text: Alas for both, both mine, Edward and Clarence!

sentences.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
sentences.text: What stay had I but Edward? And he’s gone.

sentences.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
sentences.text: What stay had I but Edward? And he’s gone.

sentences.speaker: CHILDREN
sentences.text: What stay had we but Clarence? And he’s gone.

sentences.speaker: CHILDREN
sentences.text: What stay had we but Clarence? And he’s gone.

sentences.speaker: DUCHESS
sentences.text: What stays had I but they? And they are gone.

sentences.speaker: DUCHESS
sentences.text: What stays had I but they? And they are gone.

sentences.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
sentences.text: Was never widow had so dear a loss.

sentences.speaker: CHILDREN
sentences.text: Were never orphans had so dear a loss.

sentences.speaker: DUCHESS
sentences.text:
Was never mother had so dear a loss.
Alas, I am the mother of these griefs.
Their woes are parceled; mine are general.
She for an Edward weeps, and so do I;
I for a Clarence weep; so doth not she.
These babes for Clarence weep and so do I;
I for an Edward weep; so do not they.
Alas, you three, on me, threefold distressed,
Pour all your tears. I am your sorrow’s nurse,
And I will pamper it with lamentations.

sentences.speaker: DORSET
sentences.text:
[to QUEEN ELIZABETH] Comfort, dear mother. God is
much displeased
That you take with unthankfulness, his doing.
In common worldly things, ’tis called ungrateful
With dull unwillingness to repay a debt
Which with a bounteous hand was kindly lent;
Much more to be thus opposite with heaven,
For it requires the royal debt it lent you.

sentences.speaker: RIVERS
sentences.text:
Madam, bethink you, like a careful mother,
Of the young prince your son. Send straight for him.
Let him be crowned. In him your comfort lives.
Drown desperate sorrow in dead Edward’s grave
And plant your joys in living Edward’s throne.

Enter RICHARD, BUCKINGHAM, STANLEY, HASTINGS, and RATCLIFFE

sentences.speaker: RICHARD
sentences.text:
Sister, have comfort. All of us have cause
To wail the dimming of our shining star,
But none can help our harms by wailing them.—
Madam, my mother, I do cry you mercy;
I did not see your Grace. Humbly on my knee
I crave your blessing.

sentences.speaker: RICHARD

I crave your blessing.

He kneels

sentences.speaker: DUCHESS
sentences.text:
God bless thee, and put meekness in thy breast,
Love, charity, obedience, and true duty.

sentences.speaker: RICHARD
sentences.text:
[standing] Amen. [aside] And make me die a good old
man!
That is the butt end of a mother’s blessing;
I marvel that her Grace did leave it out.

sentences.speaker: RICHARD

sentences.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
sentences.text:
You cloudy princes and heart-sorrowing peers
That bear this heavy mutual load of moan,
Now cheer each other in each other’s love.
Though we have spent our harvest of this king,
We are to reap the harvest of his son.
The broken rancor of your high-swoll'n hates,
But lately splintered, knit, and joined together,
Must gently be preserved, cherished, and kept.
Meseemeth good that with some little train
Forthwith from Ludlow the young prince be fet
Hither to London, to be crowned our king.

sentences.speaker: RIVERS
sentences.text: Why “with some little train,” my Lord of Buckingham?

sentences.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
sentences.text:
Marry, my lord, lest by a multitude
The new-healed wound of malice should break out,
Which would be so much the more dangerous
By how much the estate is green and yet ungoverned.
Where every horse bears his commanding rein
And may direct his course as please himself,
As well the fear of harm as harm apparent,
In my opinion, ought to be prevented.

sentences.speaker: RICHARD
sentences.text:
I hope the king made peace with all of us;
And the compact is firm and true in me.

sentences.speaker: RICHARD
sentences.text:
I hope the king made peace with all of us;
And the compact is firm and true in me.

sentences.speaker: RIVERS
sentences.text:
And so in me, and so, I think, in all.
Yet since it is but green, it should be put
To no apparent likelihood of breach,
Which haply by much company might be urged.
Therefore I say with noble Buckingham
That it is meet so few should fetch the prince.

sentences.speaker: RIVERS
sentences.text:

sentences.speaker: HASTINGS

sentences.speaker: RICHARD
sentences.text:
Then be it so, and go we to determine
Who they shall be that straight shall post to Ludlow.—
Madam, and you, my sister, will you go
To give your censures in this business?

sentences.speaker: RICHARD
sentences.text:
Then be it so, and go we to determine
Who they shall be that straight shall post to Ludlow.—
Madam, and you, my sister, will you go
To give your censures in this business?
Exeunt all but BUCKINGHAM and RICHARD

sentences.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
sentences.text:
My lord, whoever journeys to the prince,
For God’s sake let not us two at home.
For by the way I’ll sort occasion,
As index to the story we late talked of,
To part the queen’s proud kindred from the prince.

sentences.speaker: BUCKINGHAM

sentences.speaker: RICHARD
sentences.text:
My other self, my council’s consistory,
My oracle, my prophet, my dear cousin,
I, as a child, will go by thy direction
Toward Ludlow then, for we’ll not stay behind.

Enter one CITIZEN at one door, and another at the other

sentences.speaker: FIRST CITIZEN
sentences.text: Good morrow, neighbor, whither away so fast?

sentences.speaker: FIRST CITIZEN
sentences.text: Good morrow, neighbor, whither away so fast?

sentences.speaker: SECOND CITIZEN
sentences.text:
I promise you I scarcely know myself.
Hear you the news abroad?

sentences.speaker: SECOND CITIZEN
sentences.text:
I promise you I scarcely know myself.
Hear you the news abroad?

sentences.speaker: FIRST CITIZEN
sentences.text: Yes, that the king is dead.

sentences.speaker: SECOND CITIZEN
sentences.text:
Ill news, by 'r Lady. Seldom comes the better.
I fear, I fear, ’twill prove a giddy world.

sentences.speaker: SECOND CITIZEN
sentences.text:
Ill news, by 'r Lady. Seldom comes the better.
I fear, I fear, ’twill prove a giddy world.

Enter THIRD CITIZEN

sentences.speaker: THIRD CITIZEN
sentences.text: Neighbors, God speed.

sentences.speaker: FIRST CITIZEN
sentences.text: Give you good morrow, sir.

sentences.speaker: THIRD CITIZEN
sentences.text: Doth this news hold of good King Edward’s death?

sentences.speaker: SECOND CITIZEN
sentences.text: Ay, sir, it is too true, God help the while.

sentences.speaker: THIRD CITIZEN
sentences.text: Then, masters, look to see a troublous world.

sentences.speaker: FIRST CITIZEN
sentences.text: No, no, by God’s good grace, his son shall reign.

sentences.speaker: THIRD CITIZEN
sentences.text: Woe to the land that’s governed by a child.

sentences.speaker: SECOND CITIZEN
sentences.text:
In him there is a hope of government,
Which, in his nonage, council under him,
And, in his full and ripened years, himself,
No doubt shall then, and till then, govern well.

sentences.speaker: SECOND CITIZEN
sentences.text:

Which, in his nonage, council under him,
And, in his full and ripened years, himself,
No doubt shall then, and till then, govern well.

sentences.speaker: FIRST CITIZEN
sentences.text:
So stood the state when Henry the Sixth
Was crowned in Paris but at nine months old.

sentences.speaker: THIRD CITIZEN
sentences.text:
Stood the state so? No, no, good friends, God wot,
For then this land was famously enriched
With politic grave counsel; then the king
Had virtuous uncles to protect his Grace.

sentences.speaker: THIRD CITIZEN
sentences.text:
Stood the state so? No, no, good friends, God wot,
For then this land was famously enriched
With politic grave counsel; then the king
Had virtuous uncles to protect his Grace.

sentences.speaker: FIRST CITIZEN
sentences.text: Why, so hath this, both by the father and mother.

sentences.speaker: THIRD CITIZEN
sentences.text:
Better it were they all came by his father,
Or by the father there were none at all,
For emulation who shall now be nearest
Will touch us all too near if God prevent not.
O, full of danger is the duke of Gloucester,
And the queen’s sons and brothers haught and proud,
And were they to be ruled, and not to rule,
This sickly land might solace as before.

sentences.speaker: FIRST CITIZEN
sentences.text: Come, come, we fear the worst. All will be well.

sentences.speaker: FIRST CITIZEN

sentences.speaker: THIRD CITIZEN
THIRD CITIZEN
THIRD CITIZEN

SECOND CITIZEN
sentences.text:
Truly, the hearts of men are full of fear.
Ye cannot reason almost with a man
That looks not heavily and full of dread.

sentences.speaker: SECOND CITIZEN
sentences.text:
Truly, the hearts of men are full of fear.
Ye cannot reason almost with a man
That looks not heavily and full of dread.

sentences.speaker: THIRD CITIZEN
sentences.text:
Before the days of change, still is it so.
By a divine instinct, men’s minds mistrust
Ensuing dangers, as by proof we see
The water swell before a boist'rous storm.
But leave it all to God. Whither away?

sentences.speaker: SECOND CITIZEN

sentences.speaker: THIRD CITIZEN

Enter the ARCHBISHOP of York, the young duke of YORK, QUEEN ELIZABETH, and the old DUCHESS of York

sentences.speaker: ARCHBISHOP
sentences.text:
Last night, I hear, they lay at Stony Stratford,
And at Northampton they do rest tonight.
Tomorrow or next day they will be here.

sentences.speaker: ARCHBISHOP
sentences.text:
Last night, I hear, they lay at Stony Stratford,
And at Northampton they do rest tonight.
Tomorrow or next day they will be here.

sentences.speaker: DUCHESS
sentences.text:
I long with all my heart to see the prince.
I hope he is much grown since last I saw him.

sentences.speaker: DUCHESS
sentences.text:
I long with all my heart to see the prince.
I hope he is much grown since last I saw him.

sentences.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
sentences.text:
But I hear no; they say my son of York
Has almost overta'en him in his growth.

sentences.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
sentences.text:
But I hear no; they say my son of York
Has almost overta'en him in his growth.

sentences.speaker: YORK
sentences.text: Ay, mother, but I would not have it so.

sentences.speaker: DUCHESS
sentences.text: Why, my young cousin? It is good to grow.

sentences.speaker: DUCHESS
sentences.text: Why, my young cousin? It is good to grow.

sentences.speaker: YORK
sentences.text:
Grandam, one night as we did sit at supper,
My uncle Rivers talked how I did grow
More than my brother: “Ay,” quoth my uncle Gloucester,
“Small herbs have grace; great weeds do grow apace.”
And since, methinks I would not grow so fast
Because sweet flowers are slow and weeds make haste.

sentences.speaker: YORK
sentences.text:
Grandam, one night as we did sit at supper,
My uncle Rivers talked how I did grow
More than my brother: “Ay,” quoth my uncle Gloucester,
“Small herbs have grace; great weeds do grow apace.”
And since, methinks I would not grow so fast
Because sweet flowers are slow and weeds make haste.

sentences.speaker: DUCHESS
sentences.text:
Good faith, good faith, the saying did not hold
In him that did object the same to thee!
He was the wretched’st thing when he was young,
So long a-growing and so leisurely,
That if this rule were true, he should be gracious.

sentences.speaker: DUCHESS
sentences.text:
Good faith, good faith, the saying did not hold
In him that did object the same to thee!
He was the wretched’st thing when he was young,
So long a-growing and so leisurely,
That if this rule were true, he should be gracious.

sentences.speaker: YORK
sentences.text: And so no doubt he is, my gracious madam.

sentences.speaker: DUCHESS
sentences.text: I hope he is, but yet let mothers doubt.

sentences.speaker: YORK
sentences.text:
Now, by my troth, if I had been remembered,
I could have given my uncle’s grace a flout
To touch his growth nearer than he touched mine.

sentences.speaker: DUCHESS
sentences.text: How, my pretty York? I prithee let me hear it.

sentences.speaker: DUCHESS
sentences.text: How, my pretty York? I prithee let me hear it.

sentences.speaker: YORK
sentences.text:
Marry, they say my uncle grew so fast
That he could gnaw a crust at two hours old.
'Twas full two years ere I could get a tooth.
Grandam, this would have been a biting jest.

sentences.speaker: YORK
sentences.text:

That he could gnaw a crust at two hours old.

Grandam, this would have been a biting jest.

Grandam, this would have been a biting jest.

sentences.speaker: DUCHESS
sentences.text: I prithee, pretty York, who told thee this?

sentences.speaker: YORK
sentences.text: Grandam, his nurse.

sentences.speaker: DUCHESS
sentences.text: His nurse? Why, she was dead ere thou wast born.

sentences.speaker: DUCHESS
sentences.text: His nurse? Why, she was dead ere thou wast born.

sentences.speaker: YORK
sentences.text: If ’twere not she, I cannot tell who told me.

sentences.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
sentences.text: A parlous boy! Go to, you are too shrewd.

sentences.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
sentences.text: A parlous boy! Go to, you are too shrewd.

sentences.speaker: DUCHESS
sentences.text: Good madam, be not angry with the child.

sentences.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
sentences.text: Pitchers have ears.
Enter a MESSENGER

sentences.speaker: ARCHBISHOP
sentences.text: Here comes a messenger. —What news?

sentences.speaker: MESSENGER
sentences.text: Such news, my lord, as grieves me to report.

sentences.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
sentences.text: How doth the prince?

sentences.speaker: MESSENGER
sentences.text: Well, madam, and in health.

sentences.speaker: DUCHESS
sentences.text: What is thy news then?

sentences.speaker: MESSENGER
sentences.text:
Lord Rivers and Lord Grey are sent to Pomfret,
And, with them, Sir Thomas Vaughan, prisoners.

sentences.speaker: MESSENGER
sentences.text:
Lord Rivers and Lord Grey are sent to Pomfret,
And, with them, Sir Thomas Vaughan, prisoners.

sentences.speaker: DUCHESS
sentences.text: Who hath committed them?

sentences.speaker: MESSENGER
sentences.text: The mighty dukes, Gloucester and Buckingham.

sentences.speaker: ARCHBISHOP
sentences.text: For what offence?

sentences.speaker: MESSENGER
sentences.text:
The sum of all I can, I have disclosed.
Why, or for what, the nobles were committed
Is all unknown to me, my gracious lord.

sentences.speaker: MESSENGER
sentences.text:
The sum of all I can, I have disclosed.
Why, or for what, the nobles were committed
Is all unknown to me, my gracious lord.

sentences.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
sentences.text:
Ay me! I see the ruin of my house.
The tiger now hath seized the gentle hind.
Insulting tyranny begins to jut
Upon the innocent and aweless throne.
Welcome, destruction, blood, and massacre.
I see, as in a map, the end of all.

sentences.speaker: DUCHESS
sentences.text:
Accursèd and unquiet wrangling days,
How many of you have mine eyes beheld?
My husband lost his life to get the crown,
And often up and down my sons were tossed
For me to joy, and weep, their gain and loss.
And being seated, and domestic broils
Clean overblown, themselves the conquerors
Make war upon themselves, brother to brother,
Blood to blood, self against self. O, preposterous
And frantic outrage, end thy damnèd spleen,
Or let me die, to look on death no more.

sentences.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
sentences.text:
[to YORK] Come, come, my boy. We will to sanctuary.
Madam, farewell.

sentences.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
sentences.text:
[to YORK] Come, come, my boy. We will to sanctuary.
Madam, farewell.

sentences.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
sentences.text:
[to YORK] Come, come, my boy. We will to sanctuary.
Madam, farewell.

sentences.speaker: DUCHESS
sentences.text: Stay, I will go with you.

sentences.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
sentences.text: You have no cause.

sentences.speaker: ARCHBISHOP
sentences.text:
[to QUEEN ELIZABETH]
My gracious lady, go,
And thither bear your treasure and your goods.
For my part, I’ll resign unto your Grace
The seal I keep; and so betide to me
As well I tender you and all of yours.
Go. I’ll conduct you to the sanctuary.

The trumpets sound. Enter the young PRINCE, the CARDINAL, CATESBY, and others
The trumpets sound. Enter the young PRINCE, the CARDINAL, CATESBY, and others

sentences.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
sentences.text: Welcome, sweet prince, to London, to your chamber.

sentences.speaker: RICHARD
sentences.text:
[to PRINCE] Welcome, dear cousin, my thoughts'
sovereign.
The weary way hath made you melancholy.

sentences.speaker: RICHARD
sentences.text:
[to PRINCE] Welcome, dear cousin, my thoughts'
sovereign.
The weary way hath made you melancholy.

sentences.speaker: PRINCE
sentences.text:
No, uncle, but our crosses on the way
Have made it tedious, wearisome, and heavy.
I want more uncles here to welcome me.

sentences.speaker: PRINCE
sentences.text:
No, uncle, but our crosses on the way
Have made it tedious, wearisome, and heavy.
I want more uncles here to welcome me.

sentences.speaker: RICHARD
sentences.text:
Sweet prince, the untainted virtue of your years
Hath not yet dived into the world’s deceit;
Nor more can you distinguish of a man
Than of his outward show, which, God He knows,
Seldom or never jumpeth with the heart.
Those uncles which you want were dangerous.
Your Grace attended to their sugared words
But looked not on the poison of their hearts.
God keep you from them, and from such false friends.

sentences.speaker: PRINCE
sentences.text: God keep me from false friends, but they were none.

sentences.speaker: RICHARD
sentences.text: My lord, the mayor of London comes to greet you.
Enter the LORD MAYOR and his train

sentences.speaker: LORD MAYOR
sentences.text: God bless your Grace with health and happy days.

sentences.speaker: PRINCE
sentences.text:
I thank you, good my lord, and thank you all.—
I thought my mother and my brother York
Would long ere this have met us on the way.
Fie, what a slug is Hastings that he comes not
To tell us whether they will come or no!

sentences.speaker: PRINCE
sentences.text:

Would long ere this have met us on the way.
Fie, what a slug is Hastings that he comes not
To tell us whether they will come or no!

Enter HASTINGS

sentences.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
sentences.text: And in good time here comes the sweating lord.

sentences.speaker: PRINCE
sentences.text: Welcome, my lord. What, will our mother come?

sentences.speaker: PRINCE
sentences.text: Welcome, my lord. What, will our mother come?

sentences.speaker: HASTINGS
sentences.text:
On what occasion God He knows, not I,
The Queen your mother and your brother York
Have taken sanctuary. The tender prince
Would fain have come with me to meet your Grace,
But by his mother was perforce withheld.

sentences.speaker: HASTINGS
sentences.text:
On what occasion God He knows, not I,
The Queen your mother and your brother York
Have taken sanctuary. The tender prince
Would fain have come with me to meet your Grace,
But by his mother was perforce withheld.

sentences.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
sentences.text:
Fie, what an indirect and peevish course
Is this of hers! —Lord Cardinal, will your Grace
Persuade the queen to send the duke of York
Unto his princely brother presently?—
If she deny, Lord Hastings, go with him,
And from her jealous arms pluck him perforce.

sentences.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
sentences.text:
Fie, what an indirect and peevish course
Is this of hers! —Lord Cardinal, will your Grace
Persuade the queen to send the duke of York
Unto his princely brother presently?—
If she deny, Lord Hastings, go with him,
And from her jealous arms pluck him perforce.

sentences.speaker: CARDINAL
sentences.text:
My Lord of Buckingham, if my weak oratory
Can from his mother win the duke of York,
Anon expect him here; but if she be obdurate
To mild entreaties, God in heaven forbid
We should infringe the holy privilege
Of blessèd sanctuary! Not for all this land
Would I be guilty of so deep a sin.

My Lord of Buckingham, if my weak oratory
Can from his mother win the duke of York,

sentences.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
sentences.text:
You are too senseless obstinate, my lord,
Too ceremonious and traditional.
Weigh it but with the grossness of this age,
You break not sanctuary in seizing him.
The benefit thereof is always granted
To those whose dealings have deserved the place
And those who have the wit to claim the place.
This prince hath neither claimed it nor deserved it
And therefore, in mine opinion, cannot have it.
Then taking him from thence that is not there,
You break no privilege nor charter there.
Oft have I heard of sanctuary men,
But sanctuary children, ne'er till now.

sentences.speaker: CARDINAL
sentences.text:
My lord, you shall o'errule my mind for once.—
Come on, Lord Hastings, will you go with me?

sentences.speaker: HASTINGS
sentences.text: I go, my lord.

sentences.speaker: PRINCE
sentences.text: Good lords, make all the speedy haste you may.
Exeunt CARDINAL and HASTINGS

sentences.speaker: PRINCE
sentences.text:
Say, uncle Gloucester, if our brother come,
Where shall we sojourn till our coronation?

sentences.speaker: RICHARD
sentences.text:
Where it seems best unto your royal self.
If I may counsel you, some day or two
Your Highness shall repose you at the Tower;
Then where you please and shall be thought most fit
For your best health and recreation.

sentences.speaker: RICHARD
sentences.text:
Where it seems best unto your royal self.
If I may counsel you, some day or two
Your Highness shall repose you at the Tower;
Then where you please and shall be thought most fit
For your best health and recreation.

sentences.speaker: RICHARD
sentences.text:
Where it seems best unto your royal self.
If I may counsel you, some day or two
Your Highness shall repose you at the Tower;
Then where you please and shall be thought most fit
For your best health and recreation.

sentences.speaker: PRINCE
sentences.text:
I do not like the Tower, of any place.—
Did Julius Caesar build that place, my lord?

sentences.speaker: PRINCE
sentences.text:
I do not like the Tower, of any place.—
Did Julius Caesar build that place, my lord?

sentences.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
sentences.text:
He did, my gracious lord, begin that place,
Which, since, succeeding ages have re-edified.

sentences.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
sentences.text:
He did, my gracious lord, begin that place,
Which, since, succeeding ages have re-edified.

sentences.speaker: PRINCE
sentences.text:
Is it upon record, or else reported
Successively from age to age, he built it?

sentences.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
sentences.text: Upon record, my gracious lord.

sentences.speaker: PRINCE
sentences.text:
But say, my lord, it were not registered,
Methinks the truth should live from age to age,
As ’twere retailed to all posterity,
Even to the general all-ending day.

sentences.speaker: RICHARD
sentences.text: [aside] So wise so young, they say, do never live long.

sentences.speaker: PRINCE
sentences.text: What say you, uncle?

sentences.speaker: RICHARD
sentences.text:
I say, without characters fame lives long.
[aside] Thus, like the formal Vice, Iniquity,
I moralize two meanings in one word.

sentences.speaker: PRINCE
sentences.text:
That Julius Caesar was a famous man.
With what his valor did enrich his wit,
His wit set down to make his valor live.
Death makes no conquest of this conqueror,
For now he lives in fame, though not in life.
I’ll tell you what, my cousin Buckingham—

sentences.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
sentences.text: What, my gracious lord?

sentences.speaker: PRINCE
sentences.text:
An if I live until I be a man,
I’ll win our ancient right in France again
Or die a soldier, as I lived a king.

sentences.speaker: PRINCE
sentences.text:
An if I live until I be a man,
I’ll win our ancient right in France again
Or die a soldier, as I lived a king.

sentences.speaker: RICHARD
sentences.text: [aside] Short summers lightly have a forward spring.

sentences.speaker: RICHARD
sentences.text: [aside] Short summers lightly have a forward spring.
Enter young YORK, HASTINGS, and the CARDINAL

sentences.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
sentences.text: Now in good time here comes the duke of York.

sentences.speaker: PRINCE
sentences.text: Richard of York, how fares our loving brother?

sentences.speaker: YORK
sentences.text: Well, my dread lord—so must I call you now.

sentences.speaker: PRINCE
sentences.text:
Ay, brother, to our grief, as it is yours.
Too late he died that might have kept that title,
Which by his death hath lost much majesty.

sentences.speaker: PRINCE
sentences.text:

Too late he died that might have kept that title,
Which by his death hath lost much majesty.

sentences.speaker: RICHARD
sentences.text: How fares our cousin, noble Lord of York?

sentences.speaker: YORK
sentences.text:
I thank you, gentle uncle. O, my lord,
You said that idle weeds are fast in growth.
The prince my brother hath outgrown me far.

sentences.speaker: YORK
sentences.text:
I thank you, gentle uncle. O, my lord,
You said that idle weeds are fast in growth.
The prince my brother hath outgrown me far.

sentences.speaker: YORK
sentences.text:
I thank you, gentle uncle. O, my lord,
You said that idle weeds are fast in growth.
The prince my brother hath outgrown me far.

sentences.speaker: RICHARD
sentences.text: He hath, my lord.

sentences.speaker: YORK
sentences.text: And therefore is he idle?

sentences.speaker: RICHARD
sentences.text: O, my fair cousin, I must not say so.

sentences.speaker: YORK
sentences.text: Then is he more beholding to you than I.

sentences.speaker: RICHARD
sentences.text:
He may command me as my sovereign,
But you have power in me as in a kinsman.

sentences.speaker: YORK
sentences.text: I pray you, uncle, give me this dagger.

sentences.speaker: RICHARD
sentences.text: My dagger, little cousin? With all my heart.

sentences.speaker: RICHARD
sentences.text: My dagger, little cousin? With all my heart.

sentences.speaker: PRINCE
sentences.text: A beggar, brother?

sentences.speaker: YORK
sentences.text:
Of my kind uncle, that I know will give,
And being but a toy, which is no grief to give.

sentences.speaker: YORK
sentences.text:
Of my kind uncle, that I know will give,
And being but a toy, which is no grief to give.

sentences.speaker: RICHARD
sentences.text: A greater gift than that I’ll give my cousin.

sentences.speaker: YORK
sentences.text: A greater gift? O, that’s the sword to it.

sentences.speaker: YORK
sentences.text: A greater gift? O, that’s the sword to it.

sentences.speaker: RICHARD
sentences.text: Ay, gentle cousin, were it light enough.

sentences.speaker: RICHARD
sentences.text: Ay, gentle cousin, were it light enough.

sentences.speaker: YORK
sentences.text:
O, then I see you will part but with light gifts.
In weightier things you’ll say a beggar nay.

sentences.speaker: YORK
sentences.text:
O, then I see you will part but with light gifts.
In weightier things you’ll say a beggar nay.

sentences.speaker: RICHARD
sentences.text: It is too heavy for your Grace to wear.

sentences.speaker: YORK
sentences.text: I weigh it lightly, were it heavier.

sentences.speaker: RICHARD
sentences.text: What, would you have my weapon, little lord?

sentences.speaker: YORK
sentences.text: I would, that I might thank you as you call me.

sentences.speaker: RICHARD
sentences.text: How?

sentences.speaker: YORK
sentences.text: Little.

sentences.speaker: PRINCE
sentences.text:
My lord of York will still be cross in talk.
Uncle, your Grace knows how to bear with him.

sentences.speaker: PRINCE
sentences.text:
My lord of York will still be cross in talk.
Uncle, your Grace knows how to bear with him.

sentences.speaker: YORK
sentences.text:
You mean, to bear me, not to bear with me.—
Uncle, my brother mocks both you and me.
Because that I am little, like an ape,
He thinks that you should bear me on your shoulders.

sentences.speaker: YORK
sentences.text:
You mean, to bear me, not to bear with me.—
Uncle, my brother mocks both you and me.
Because that I am little, like an ape,
He thinks that you should bear me on your shoulders.

sentences.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
sentences.text:
[aside] With what a sharp-provided wit he reasons!
To mitigate the scorn he gives his uncle,
He prettily and aptly taunts himself.
So cunning and so young is wonderful.

sentences.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
sentences.text:
[aside] With what a sharp-provided wit he reasons!

He prettily and aptly taunts himself.
